enrichment.seo_title: Harness Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Harness is an AI-native software delivery platform for CI/CD, feature flags, security testing and cost management. See features, pricing, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Harness is an AI-native software delivery platform that consolidates continuous integration, continuous delivery, feature flag management, security testing, chaos engineering, infrastructure as code, and cloud cost management into a single modular product suite. Rather than positioning itself as a point solution, Harness aims to be the system of record for the entire software delivery lifecycle, letting engineering organizations replace a patchwork of CI runners, deployment scripts, flag services and cost dashboards with one connected platform that shares pipelines, governance policies, and audit trails across every stage of shipping code. The platform is built around the idea that delivery should be safe by default, using automated verification, canary analysis and rollback logic to catch bad deployments before they reach end users, and more recently it has layered generative AI assistance on top of pipelines to auto-generate YAML, summarize failures, and recommend fixes. Harness was founded in 2017 in San Francisco by Jyoti Bansal and Rishi Singh, and was incubated inside BIG Labs, a startup studio Bansal created after selling his previous company, AppDynamics, to Cisco for 3.7 billion dollars. Bansal, a serial enterprise infrastructure entrepreneur who also co-founded the API security company Traceable and the venture firm Unusual Ventures, set out with Harness to give the world's tens of millions of software developers a way to deliver code quickly, reliably, efficiently and securely, explicitly framing the product as the spiritual successor to the continuous delivery tooling he had built internally at AppDynamics. Rishi Singh, the founding CTO, had previously worked on DevOps and release engineering initiatives at Apple before joining Harness to lead its early engineering team. The company has since become one of the most heavily funded independent DevOps vendors, raising roughly 570 million to 815 million dollars across multiple rounds depending on how funding is tallied, from investors including Battery Ventures, Menlo Ventures, Norwest Venture Partners, Google Ventures (GV) and Goldman Sachs Alternatives, and reported figures put its valuation in the multi-billion-dollar range with annualized recurring revenue exceeding 150 million dollars. Harness remains an independent, privately held company with more than 1,000 customers and over 20 offices worldwide. Architecturally, Harness is built as a Kubernetes-native, microservices-based SaaS platform (with a self-managed enterprise option) organized around discrete but interoperable modules: Continuous Integration (CI) for building and testing code, Continuous Delivery and GitOps (CD) for deploying to Kubernetes, VM, serverless and traditional targets, Feature Flags for progressive delivery and experimentation, Security Testing Orchestration (STO) for aggregating and de-duplicating results from static and dynamic scanners, Chaos Engineering (built on the open-source LitmusChaos project Harness maintains) for resilience testing, Cloud Cost Management (CCM) for Kubernetes and cloud spend visibility and automated rightsizing, Service Reliability Management (SRM) for SLO tracking, and Infrastructure as Code Management (IaCM) for governing Terraform and OpenTofu workflows. All modules share a common pipeline engine, role-based access control model, audit log, and policy-as-code layer (built on Open Policy Agent), which is the core differentiator Harness leans on versus assembling Jenkins, Spinnaker, LaunchDarkly, Snyk and CloudHealth separately. On the AI side, Harness has introduced an AI Test Automation Agent, an AI-powered code review capability (branded around its 'Developer 360' vision), and generative pipeline authoring, positioning itself increasingly as an 'AI-native' vendor for the software delivery lifecycle rather than a traditional CI/CD tool. Pricing is modular and, since a 2024 to 2025 overhaul, priced per developer rather than per build minute or per seat-by-module, which Harness says simplifies budgeting for engineering leaders. The Free plan offers open-source starter capabilities (including Harness CI, Harness Open Source and Litmus Chaos) intended for individual developers and small teams experimenting with the platform. The Essentials plan bundles core modules such as CI, CD, Code Repository, STO and IaCM for growing organizations, supporting up to 500 users, one organization, five custom dashboards and 60 concurrent pipeline executions, with standard support; pricing is quote-based rather than published. The Enterprise plan removes those caps entirely, offers the full module catalog on an a-la-carte basis, premium support with a dedicated account manager, and an optional on-premises or self-managed deployment add-on for regulated industries. Industry pricing trackers report a median annual contract value around 49,000 dollars, with multi-year commitments commonly earning 15 to 30 percent discounts. Harness's target audience spans mid-market to large enterprise platform engineering and DevOps teams, particularly organizations running Kubernetes at scale that have outgrown a stitched-together toolchain of Jenkins, ArgoCD, LaunchDarkly, Snyk, and manual FinOps spreadsheets. Its closest competitors include GitLab and GitHub (which bundle CI/CD into their broader DevOps platforms), CircleCI and Jenkins for pure CI, Spinnaker and Argo CD for deployment, LaunchDarkly for feature flags, and CloudHealth or Kubecost for cost management; Harness differentiates by offering all of these as natively integrated modules on one control plane with shared governance and a single bill, rather than requiring customers to integrate and maintain several best-of-breed vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Harness is a modular software delivery platform that combines CI/CD, feature flags, security testing orchestration, chaos engineering, cloud cost management and infrastructure as code governance under one roof. Founded in 2017 by Jyoti Bansal (co-founder of AppDynamics) and Rishi Singh, it was built to give engineering organizations a single, policy-governed pipeline engine instead of stitching together separate point tools for every stage of the delivery lifecycle.

The platform runs on Kubernetes and is available as SaaS or as a self-managed enterprise deployment. Each module (CI, CD/GitOps, Feature Flags, STO, CCM, SRM, Chaos Engineering, IaCM) can be adopted independently but shares a common pipeline engine, RBAC model, audit trail and policy-as-code layer, which is the core reason large engineering teams consolidate onto Harness rather than running Jenkins, Argo CD, LaunchDarkly and Snyk separately.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Harness Continuous Integration provides cloud-hosted or self-hosted build infrastructure with caching, test intelligence (running only tests affected by a code change), and parallelization to cut build times. Harness Continuous Delivery and GitOps supports Kubernetes, VM, serverless and traditional deployment targets with built-in canary analysis, automated rollback and native Argo CD-based GitOps workflows.

Feature Flags enables progressive delivery and A/B testing without redeploying code, while Security Testing Orchestration aggregates, de-duplicates and prioritizes findings from static analysis, dynamic analysis and software composition analysis tools into one governance view. Chaos Engineering, built on the open-source LitmusChaos project, lets teams run controlled resilience experiments against production-like environments.

Cloud Cost Management gives visibility into Kubernetes and cloud spend with automated rightsizing and commitment planning, Service Reliability Management tracks SLOs and error budgets, and Infrastructure as Code Management governs Terraform and OpenTofu plans with policy checks before they apply. Generative AI features layered across these modules can draft pipeline YAML, summarize failed builds, and suggest fixes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Harness prices per developer rather than per build minute, with three tiers. The Free plan gives individuals and small teams access to open-source starter capabilities including Harness CI and Litmus Chaos at no cost. The Essentials plan bundles core modules (CI, CD, Code Repository, STO, IaCM) for growing teams, capped at 500 users, one organization and 60 concurrent pipeline executions, priced via a sales quote.

The Enterprise plan removes all caps, unlocks the full module catalog, and adds premium support with a dedicated account manager plus an optional on-premises deployment add-on. Published third-party pricing data puts the median annual contract around 49,000 dollars, with multi-year prepayment commonly discounted 15 to 30 percent.

faqs.question: What is Harness used for
faqs.answer: Harness is used to automate and govern software delivery, covering continuous integration, continuous deployment, feature flag rollouts, security scan orchestration, chaos testing and cloud cost optimization from one platform.

faqs.question: Is Harness free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Harness offers a Free plan with open-source starter capabilities such as Harness CI and Litmus Chaos. Paid Essentials and Enterprise plans unlock higher usage limits and additional modules.

faqs.question: Who founded Harness
faqs.answer: Harness was founded in 2017 by Jyoti Bansal and Rishi Singh, incubated inside Bansal's startup studio BIG Labs after he sold his previous company AppDynamics to Cisco.

faqs.question: How is Harness priced
faqs.answer: Harness uses per-developer modular pricing across three tiers: Free, Essentials and Enterprise. Essentials and Enterprise pricing is quote-based and depends on which modules and usage limits an organization needs.

faqs.question: Does Harness replace Jenkins
faqs.answer: Harness can replace Jenkins for continuous integration and adds continuous delivery, feature flags, security testing and other modules on top, though migrating existing Jenkins pipelines requires reconfiguration.

faqs.question: What is Harness Chaos Engineering built on
faqs.answer: Harness Chaos Engineering is built on LitmusChaos, an open-source chaos engineering project that Harness maintains and contributes to.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Harvest is a time tracking and invoicing tool for freelancers and teams. Read our 2026 review covering features, pricing plans, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Harvest is a web-based time tracking, invoicing and expense management platform built for freelancers, agencies and small-to-midsize teams that need to understand where billable hours go and turn tracked time into client invoices. Users log time from a browser, desktop app (Mac and Windows), mobile app (iOS and Android) or browser extension, categorize it against clients and projects, and Harvest converts that data into visual reports on project profitability, team capacity and budget burn, plus one-click invoices that can be paid online through integrations with Stripe, PayPal and other processors. The product's central pitch has always been simplicity: rather than being a sprawling project management suite, Harvest focuses tightly on the time-to-invoice workflow and connects outward to tools like Asana, Trello, Slack, QuickBooks, Xero and Deel rather than trying to replace them. Harvest was created in 2006 by Danny Wen and Shawn Liu, who were running a New York City web design consultancy called Iridesco and, like many services businesses, struggled to track billable hours and invoice clients accurately. After roughly four months of development on an early version of Ruby on Rails, they launched Harvest as a side project to solve their own problem, and it grew into a standalone SaaS business that both founders eventually ran full time. Notably, Harvest built a large, profitable customer base, reportedly exceeding 40,000 organizations, entirely through organic growth and word of mouth without ever raising venture capital, making it one of the more prominent bootstrapped success stories in the productivity software category. That changed in June 2025, when Harvest was acquired by Bending Spoons, the Milan-based technology holding company known for acquiring and operationally overhauling consumer and productivity apps such as Evernote, Meetup, WeTransfer and Vimeo. Since the acquisition, Bending Spoons has restructured Harvest's pricing model, moving from a flat per-seat fee to a base rate plus usage-based charges tied to invoices, projects, clients and tasks created, a change that has drawn criticism from long-time users on review sites reporting substantial cost increases for high-usage accounts. Technically, Harvest is a straightforward multi-platform SaaS product: a central web application with native Mac and Windows desktop timers, iOS and Android mobile apps, and a browser extension, all syncing time entries and integrating with accounting platforms via API and prebuilt connectors, plus a public API for custom integrations. Core features include one-click and idle-detection timers, visual project budget and profitability dashboards, customizable and exportable reports, professional invoice generation with online payment collection, receipt-scanning expense tracking, timesheet approval workflows for managers, and (on higher tiers) single sign-on and detailed activity logging for compliance-conscious organizations. Pricing today spans four tiers. The Free plan supports one seat and two projects with core time tracking, expense tracking and invoicing at no cost, aimed at solo freelancers testing the product. The Teams plan costs 9 dollars per seat per month billed monthly, or 108 dollars per seat annually (a 20 percent discount), and adds unlimited seats plus team reporting and accounting and payment integrations. The Enterprise plan costs 14 dollars per seat per month, or 168 dollars annually, and layers on unlimited custom reports, profitability reporting, timesheet approvals, SAML-based single sign-on and an activity log for audit purposes. An Enterprise Plus tier above that offers custom-negotiated pricing, custom integrations, product customization, white-labeling, a dedicated success manager and assisted data migration for large organizations with bespoke requirements; all paid tiers include a 30-day free trial. Harvest's target audience is squarely freelancers, consultancies, creative and marketing agencies, and professional services firms that bill clients by the hour and need to keep a tight loop between time logged and invoices sent, rather than large enterprises needing full resource-management or ERP capability. Its most direct competitors are Toggl Track, Clockify, TimeCamp and, for the agency-focused profitability angle, tools like Everhour and Productive.io; Harvest differentiates through its long track record of reliability, clean and minimal interface, deep accounting integrations, and its historical reputation (now complicated somewhat by the Bending Spoons ownership change and pricing restructure) as a dependable, low-friction tool built by people solving their own agency problem rather than a venture-funded growth-at-all-costs product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Harvest is a time tracking, invoicing and expense management tool created in 2006 by Danny Wen and Shawn Liu to solve billing problems at their own New York City web design agency, Iridesco. It grew into a widely used bootstrapped SaaS product before being acquired by Bending Spoons in June 2025.

The product focuses narrowly on the time-to-invoice workflow: log hours against clients and projects from web, desktop, mobile or browser extension apps, review profitability and budget-burn reports, and generate invoices that clients can pay online, rather than trying to be a full project management suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include one-click and idle-detection timers, visual project budget and profitability dashboards, and customizable exportable reports for team and client-level analysis. Invoicing supports online payment collection through Stripe, PayPal and other processors, and expense tracking allows receipt photo uploads from the mobile app.

Higher tiers add timesheet approval workflows for managers, SAML-based single sign-on, and detailed activity logs for compliance. Harvest integrates with project tools like Asana, Trello and Slack, and accounting platforms including QuickBooks, Xero and Deel, plus a public API for custom connections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Harvest offers a permanently free plan for one seat and two projects. The Teams plan costs 9 dollars per seat per month (108 dollars per seat annually with a 20 percent discount) and adds unlimited seats and team reporting. The Enterprise plan costs 14 dollars per seat per month (168 dollars annually) and adds profitability reporting, approvals and SSO.

Since the 2025 Bending Spoons acquisition, paid plans also carry usage-based charges layered on top of the per-seat base rate for additional invoices, projects, clients and tasks. An Enterprise Plus tier offers custom pricing with white-labeling and a dedicated success manager for large organizations, and all paid plans include a 30-day free trial.

enrichment.seo_title: Harvester Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Harvester is an open-source hyperconverged infrastructure platform built on Kubernetes. See its features, licensing, pros, cons and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Harvester is an open-source hyperconverged infrastructure (HCI) platform that lets organizations run both virtual machines and containerized workloads on bare metal servers using entirely open-source components, positioning itself as a free alternative to proprietary HCI and virtualization stacks like VMware vSphere and Nutanix. Rather than building a new hypervisor from scratch, Harvester is assembled from proven cloud-native building blocks: Kubernetes for orchestration, KubeVirt for running full virtual machines as Kubernetes-native resources, Longhorn for distributed block storage, and Linux with KVM underneath for the actual virtualization layer. The result is a single installable appliance that gives operators a VMware-like experience, complete with a web UI for managing VMs, networks and storage, while everything underneath is standard open-source infrastructure with no proprietary kernel or licensing lock-in. Harvester was announced by SUSE's Rancher engineering team in 2020, shortly after SUSE completed its acquisition of Rancher Labs in December of that year. Rancher Labs had built its reputation on Rancher, a widely used open-source Kubernetes cluster management platform, and Harvester extended that lineage downward into the infrastructure layer itself: instead of assuming customers already had virtualized infrastructure to deploy Kubernetes onto, Harvester lets them build that infrastructure layer directly on bare metal using the same cloud-native tooling philosophy. Because it originated inside SUSE (and before that, Rancher Labs, headquartered in Cupertino, California before the acquisition), Harvester today is stewarded by SUSE's Rancher business unit, with SUSE itself operating out of Nuremberg, Germany as its primary operational headquarters. The project is fully open source, hosted on GitHub under an Apache 2.0-style open governance model, with active contributions from SUSE engineers and the broader community, and it integrates tightly with Rancher for multi-cluster Kubernetes management, letting administrators provision downstream Kubernetes clusters directly from VMs running inside Harvester. Technically, Harvester requires no prior Kubernetes knowledge to operate: administrators install it directly onto bare metal servers via an ISO-based installer, after which it presents a management dashboard for creating and migrating VMs, configuring networking (including VLANs and multiple network interfaces), and managing distributed storage pools backed by Longhorn, which replicates VM disk data across nodes for resilience without requiring a separate SAN or NAS appliance. Because VMs run as KubeVirt-managed pods under the hood, the same cluster can simultaneously host traditional virtualized workloads (Windows or Linux VMs migrated from legacy hypervisors) and native containerized applications, making Harvester a practical bridge for organizations modernizing legacy virtual machine estates toward Kubernetes without a disruptive, all-at-once migration. Typical deployment scenarios include data center consolidation projects replacing end-of-life or cost-prohibitive VMware licenses, edge computing sites that need a lightweight, self-contained HCI stack on a small number of physical nodes, and platform teams that want a single infrastructure layer capable of running both legacy VM-based applications and new cloud-native services. Because Harvester is completely open source with no per-CPU or per-socket licensing fees, the cost model is fundamentally different from commercial HCI: there is no purchase price, subscription or seat fee for the software itself, and organizations only pay for the bare-metal hardware they run it on plus, optionally, commercial support. SUSE offers paid consulting, migration assistance and enterprise support contracts (often bundled with SUSE's broader Rancher and virtualization offerings) for organizations that want SLA-backed assistance rather than relying on community Slack and GitHub issue support, but there is no requirement to purchase anything to deploy and run Harvester in production. Harvester's target audience is infrastructure and platform engineering teams at organizations looking to escape rising VMware and Broadcom licensing costs following Broadcom's 2023 acquisition of VMware, as well as teams already invested in the Rancher and Kubernetes ecosystem who want a unified way to manage both VMs and containers. Its most direct competitors are OpenShift Virtualization (Red Hat's KubeVirt-based offering), Proxmox VE, and commercial HCI vendors like Nutanix and VMware vSphere/vSAN; Harvester differentiates primarily on being 100 percent open source with zero licensing cost, deep native integration with Rancher for multi-cluster management, and a design philosophy built entirely from existing, battle-tested cloud-native components rather than a proprietary hypervisor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Harvester is an open-source hyperconverged infrastructure platform that runs virtual machines and containers side by side on bare metal servers. It is built from existing cloud-native components, Kubernetes, KubeVirt, Longhorn and KVM, rather than a proprietary hypervisor, and was announced by SUSE's Rancher engineering team in 2020 following SUSE's acquisition of Rancher Labs.

The project positions itself as a free, open-source alternative to VMware vSphere and Nutanix, aimed at organizations that want to consolidate virtualization and Kubernetes infrastructure onto one platform without per-CPU licensing fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Harvester installs directly onto bare metal via an ISO-based installer and presents a web dashboard for managing VMs, networking and storage without requiring Kubernetes expertise. VMs run as KubeVirt-managed workloads, so the same cluster can host legacy virtualized applications and native containers simultaneously.

Longhorn provides distributed, replicated block storage for VM disks without a separate SAN appliance, and Harvester integrates natively with Rancher, letting administrators provision downstream Kubernetes clusters directly from VMs running inside the platform. Support for VLANs, multiple network interfaces and live VM migration rounds out its feature set for production data center and edge use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Harvester itself is entirely free and open source with no licensing fees, purchase price or seat cost. Organizations pay only for the underlying hardware they deploy it on.

SUSE offers optional paid enterprise support, migration assistance and consulting for organizations that want SLA-backed help rather than relying on the community Slack channel and GitHub issue tracker, but no commercial license is required to run Harvester in production.

faqs.answer: Yes, Harvester is completely free and open source with no licensing fees. Optional paid enterprise support is available from SUSE.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Harvey is an enterprise AI platform for law firms and legal teams. See its features, pricing, funding, pros, cons and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Harvey is an enterprise artificial intelligence platform purpose-built for legal and professional services work, offering domain-specific AI tools for legal research, contract analysis, document drafting, due diligence and litigation support. Rather than positioning itself as a general-purpose chatbot with a legal skin, Harvey is built around workflows that mirror how lawyers actually work: analyzing large volumes of documents, cross-referencing case law and regulation, drafting and redlining contracts, and producing memos and briefs that meet the evidentiary and citation standards legal professionals require. The platform is organized into several product modules, including Assistant for open-ended legal question answering and drafting, Vault for secure storage and bulk analysis of large legal document sets, Knowledge for researching complex legal, regulatory and tax questions across jurisdictions, Workflow Agents for automating multi-step processes like due diligence review, Contract Intelligence for extracting key terms and clauses from agreements, and a Command Center that gives firm leadership usage analytics and benchmarking to guide AI adoption. Harvey was founded in the summer of 2022 by Winston Weinberg, a former securities and antitrust litigator at law firm O'Melveny and Myers, and Gabriel Pereyra, a research scientist with a background at Google DeepMind and Meta. The pairing of a practicing litigator with a machine learning researcher shaped Harvey's core thesis from day one: general-purpose large language models were not, on their own, reliable or trustworthy enough for legal work without domain-specific fine-tuning, retrieval infrastructure and rigorous evaluation built specifically around legal accuracy and citation integrity. Harvey's earliest funding, a 5 million dollar round in November 2022, was notably led by the OpenAI Startup Fund, and the company has maintained a close model relationship with OpenAI while also incorporating models from other providers over time. Harvey's funding trajectory since then has been unusually fast even by generative AI startup standards: an 80 million dollar Series B in December 2023 led by Kleiner Perkins and Elad Gil valued the company at 715 million dollars; a 100 million dollar Series C in July 2024 led by GV (Google Ventures), with participation from OpenAI, Kleiner Perkins and Sequoia Capital, pushed the valuation to 1.5 billion dollars; and by March 2026 Harvey had raised 200 million dollars in a round co-led by GIC and Sequoia Capital at an 11 billion dollar valuation, roughly four years after founding. Total funding raised across all rounds is reported at approximately 1.22 billion dollars, from an investor roster that includes Sequoia Capital, Kleiner Perkins, Andreessen Horowitz, GV, Elad Gil and the OpenAI Startup Fund. The company is headquartered in San Francisco and reports serving more than 1,000 clients globally, including roughly half of the Am Law 100 (the 100 largest US law firms by revenue), as well as corporate legal departments, tax and accounting firms, and government agencies; by its own published figures the platform processes over 850,000 queries and more than 50 million files per day across its customer base. Harvey does not publish list pricing on its website; instead, enterprise contracts are negotiated individually based on firm size, seat count and which modules are licensed. Third-party market intelligence estimates place per-seat costs in the range of 1,000 to 1,200 dollars per month, or roughly 12,000 to 16,800 dollars per seat annually depending on whether the base package or a premium bundle is selected, with most engagements requiring a minimum of 25 to 50 or more lawyer seats. That structure typically results in total annual contract values ranging from about 30,000 dollars for smaller firm deployments up to several hundred thousand dollars or more for large Am Law 100 firms, sold through six-month-plus enterprise sales cycles rather than self-serve checkout. Harvey's target audience is squarely large law firms, in-house corporate legal departments, and other professional services organizations (accounting, tax and consulting firms) that handle high volumes of document-intensive, precision-critical work and have the budget for enterprise AI deployment with dedicated account management and custom integrations into firm document management systems. Its competitive set includes other legal-specific AI vendors such as CoCounsel (Thomson Reuters, formerly Casetext), Robin AI, Lexis+ AI, Spellbook, and Legora, as well as generic enterprise AI assistants that firms sometimes evaluate as cheaper alternatives; Harvey differentiates through its litigator-plus-researcher founding team, its deep integrations and workflow customization for large firms, its very high-profile investor base including OpenAI itself, and market-leading adoption among the largest US law firms.

enrichment.seo_title: HashiCorp Consul Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HashiCorp Consul is a service mesh and service discovery platform for multi-cloud networking. See features, pricing tiers, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HashiCorp Consul is a service networking platform that gives engineering teams a consistent way to discover, connect and secure services across on-premises data centers, hybrid infrastructure and multiple public clouds, regardless of whether those services run on virtual machines, Kubernetes or bare metal. At its core, Consul solves two intertwined problems that grow more painful as infrastructure becomes more distributed: service discovery, meaning knowing where a given service instance currently lives and whether it is healthy, and service mesh, meaning securing and controlling how services talk to each other using identity-based authorization, mutual TLS encryption, and layer-7 traffic management such as canary releases and traffic splitting. Consul was created by HashiCorp, the infrastructure software company founded in 2012 in San Francisco by Mitchell Hashimoto and Armon Dadgar, who built the company around a suite of tools addressing different layers of the cloud infrastructure lifecycle, including Terraform for provisioning, Vault for secrets management, Nomad for orchestration, and Consul specifically for networking, first released in 2014. HashiCorp grew into one of the most influential open-source infrastructure companies of the 2010s, went public on Nasdaq in December 2021, and was ultimately acquired by IBM in a deal valued at approximately 6.4 billion dollars, which closed in February 2025; Consul, along with the rest of the HashiCorp product portfolio, now operates as part of IBM while continuing to be developed and released under the HashiCorp brand and its existing licensing model. That licensing model itself shifted in August 2023, when HashiCorp moved its core products, including Consul, from the permissive Mozilla Public License 2.0 to the Business Source License (BSL) 1.1, a change that restricts competitors from offering Consul as a directly competing managed service while keeping the source available and free for internal use, a decision that prompted a portion of the open-source community to fork earlier MPL-licensed code into the competing Consul-compatible project. Architecturally, Consul runs as a distributed system of client and server agents using a gossip protocol for cluster membership and the Raft consensus algorithm for maintaining a strongly consistent catalog of services, health checks and configuration; in service mesh mode, Consul deploys sidecar proxies (commonly Envoy) alongside application instances to intercept and secure traffic without requiring application code changes, and it can also front cross-service traffic through API and ingress gateways for edge cases like exposing mesh services to external clients or connecting meshes across cluster boundaries. Consul's feature set spans service discovery and a distributed key-value store for dynamic configuration, service mesh with mutual TLS and intention-based access control lists (essentially an allow list defining which services may talk to which), Layer 7 traffic management for canary deployments and service splitting, and multi-datacenter and multi-cloud federation that lets a single mesh span AWS, Azure, GCP and on-premises infrastructure simultaneously. Consul ships as free, self-hosted open-source software (Community Edition) with no functional limits on the core service discovery and service mesh capabilities, making it a common default choice for platform teams standardizing on HashiCorp's broader tool ecosystem alongside Terraform and Vault. Beyond the free community edition, HashiCorp (now IBM) offers two commercial paths: HCP Consul Dedicated, a fully managed SaaS offering on HashiCorp Cloud Platform priced on a usage basis (historically structured around a low-cost development tier for non-production testing and a production-grade tier billed per hour plus a per-service-instance fee), and Consul Enterprise, a self-managed license deployed on the customer's own infrastructure that unlocks features like performance-focused read replicas, single sign-on via Sentinel policy-as-code, automated backups, and namespaces and admin partitions for multi-tenant isolation, priced through a custom sales quote that industry trackers report frequently lands in the low-to-mid six figures annually depending on scale. Consul's target audience is platform and infrastructure engineering teams operating distributed, often heterogeneous environments, particularly organizations running a mix of VM-based legacy services and Kubernetes workloads that need consistent service discovery and secure connectivity across both, as well as enterprises spanning multiple cloud providers or regulated industries needing zero-trust network segmentation. Its closest competitors include Istio and Linkerd for Kubernetes-native service mesh, Envoy-based control planes more broadly, AWS Cloud Map and Eureka for service discovery specifically, and cloud-provider-native mesh offerings like AWS App Mesh; Consul differentiates by working consistently across both VM and Kubernetes environments rather than being Kubernetes-only, and by integrating tightly with the rest of the HashiCorp (now IBM) toolchain, particularly Terraform for provisioning and Vault for the certificates and secrets that back its mutual TLS implementation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HashiCorp Consul is a service networking platform providing service discovery, service mesh and secure service-to-service connectivity across on-premises, hybrid and multi-cloud environments. First released in 2014 by HashiCorp, the infrastructure software company founded in 2012 by Mitchell Hashimoto and Armon Dadgar, Consul now operates as part of IBM following IBM's 6.4 billion dollar acquisition of HashiCorp, completed in February 2025.

Consul works consistently across virtual machines, bare metal and Kubernetes, which distinguishes it from Kubernetes-only service mesh options, and is commonly adopted by teams already using other HashiCorp tools like Terraform and Vault.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Consul provides service discovery and health checking via a distributed catalog backed by the Raft consensus algorithm, plus a key-value store for dynamic configuration. Its service mesh mode deploys sidecar proxies, typically Envoy, to encrypt traffic with mutual TLS and enforce identity-based intentions that define which services may communicate.

Layer 7 traffic management enables canary deployments and service splitting, and multi-datacenter federation lets a single mesh span multiple clouds and on-premises data centers. API and ingress gateways expose mesh services to external clients or connect separate meshes together.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Consul Community Edition is free, open-source and self-hosted, released under HashiCorp's Business Source License since August 2023, with no functional limits on core service discovery and service mesh capabilities.

HCP Consul Dedicated is a managed SaaS offering with usage-based pricing, historically including a low-cost development tier and a production tier billed hourly plus a per-service-instance fee. Consul Enterprise is a self-managed license with advanced features like namespaces, admin partitions and automated backups, priced through a custom sales quote.

enrichment.seo_title: HashiCorp Vault Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HashiCorp Vault is a secrets management and data protection platform. See its features, pricing tiers, pros, cons and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HashiCorp Vault is a centralized secrets management and data protection platform that lets organizations secure, store and tightly control access to tokens, passwords, API keys, certificates and encryption keys through a unified UI, command-line interface or HTTP API. Vault addresses a problem that grows more dangerous as infrastructure scales: sensitive credentials scattered across configuration files, environment variables, source code and spreadsheets create a sprawling attack surface, and Vault's answer is to centralize those secrets behind strong authentication, fine-grained access policies, and comprehensive audit logging so every request for a secret is authenticated, authorized and recorded. Vault was built by HashiCorp, the infrastructure software company founded in 2012 in San Francisco by Mitchell Hashimoto and Armon Dadgar, as part of a broader suite of infrastructure tools that also includes Terraform for provisioning, Consul for service networking, and Nomad for workload orchestration; Vault itself was first released in 2015 and quickly became one of the most widely adopted secrets management tools in the DevOps and platform engineering world, particularly for organizations running Kubernetes and multi-cloud infrastructure. HashiCorp went public on Nasdaq in December 2021 and was subsequently acquired by IBM in a deal valued at approximately 6.4 billion dollars, a transaction that closed in February 2025; Vault, like the rest of the HashiCorp portfolio, is now part of IBM's security and infrastructure offerings while continuing under the HashiCorp brand. In August 2023, HashiCorp changed the license on its core products, including Vault, from the open-source Mozilla Public License 2.0 to the Business Source License 1.1, a move that keeps source code visible and free for internal use but restricts third parties from offering Vault as a directly competing managed service, a decision that was controversial within parts of the open-source community and prompted forks of the last MPL-licensed codebase. Technically, Vault is organized around pluggable secrets engines, each handling a different category of secret: the Key-Value engine stores arbitrary static secrets, database secrets engines generate short-lived, dynamically created database credentials on demand and automatically revoke them after a lease expires, the PKI engine issues and manages short-lived TLS certificates, and the Transit engine provides encryption as a service, letting applications encrypt and decrypt data without ever handling the raw encryption keys themselves, keeping cryptographic material entirely inside Vault. Access is controlled through Vault's policy language, evaluated against the identity of the requester, which can be authenticated via numerous methods, including cloud IAM roles (AWS, Azure, GCP), Kubernetes service accounts, LDAP, GitHub, and username and password, and every secret access is written to an immutable audit log for compliance and forensics. Dynamic secrets are one of Vault's most distinctive capabilities: instead of a database password that is created once and rotated manually (or never), Vault can generate a brand-new, unique database credential for each application or session on demand, with an automatic time-to-live after which the credential is revoked, dramatically shrinking the window in which a leaked credential remains useful to an attacker. Vault is distributed in three main forms. The Community Edition is entirely free and open source, self-hosted on the customer's own infrastructure with no secret-count limits, core secrets management, dynamic secrets, the Transit encryption engine, and identity-based access control included at no charge, though after the 2023 license change it operates under the BSL 1.1 rather than a traditional OSI-approved open-source license. HCP Vault Dedicated is HashiCorp's fully managed SaaS offering on HashiCorp Cloud Platform, with a low-cost Development tier around 0.03 dollars per hour (roughly 22 dollars per month) for non-production testing with no per-client fee, an Essentials tier for production workloads with a 99.9 percent uptime SLA starting around 1,152 dollars per month for a small cluster plus roughly 72.92 dollars per authenticated client per month, and a Standard tier adding performance replication, Sentinel policy-as-code and premium support at a higher hourly rate; a separately sold HCP Vault Secrets SaaS product was discontinued in 2025, consolidating HashiCorp's cloud secrets offering around HCP Vault Dedicated. Vault Enterprise is a self-managed license deployed on the customer's own infrastructure for organizations wanting full control over topology, storage backend and network configuration, adding the Transform secrets engine for format-preserving tokenization of sensitive data, disaster recovery replication, Sentinel governance policies and namespaces for multi-tenancy; HashiCorp does not publish Enterprise pricing, and industry sources report quotes commonly starting in the low six figures annually even for relatively small deployments. Vault's target audience is DevOps, platform engineering and security teams at organizations of any size that need to eliminate hardcoded credentials and centralize secret sprawl, ranging from startups adopting the free Community Edition to regulated enterprises in finance and healthcare requiring Vault Enterprise's compliance and governance features. Its main competitors include AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault and Google Secret Manager for cloud-native teams committed to a single provider, along with newer entrants like Infisical and Doppler that target a simpler developer experience; Vault differentiates through its cloud-agnostic design, its unusually deep dynamic secrets and encryption-as-service capabilities, and its position as the de facto standard secrets manager across the wider HashiCorp and Kubernetes ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HashiCorp Vault is a secrets management platform for securely storing, generating and controlling access to tokens, passwords, certificates and encryption keys through a UI, CLI or HTTP API. First released in 2015 by HashiCorp, the company founded in 2012 by Mitchell Hashimoto and Armon Dadgar, Vault is now part of IBM following IBM's 6.4 billion dollar acquisition of HashiCorp, completed in February 2025.

Vault centralizes secrets that would otherwise be scattered across configuration files and environment variables, enforcing authentication, fine-grained access policies and comprehensive audit logging for every secret request.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Vault's pluggable secrets engines cover different secret types: a Key-Value engine for static secrets, database secrets engines that generate short-lived dynamic credentials automatically revoked after use, a PKI engine for issuing short-lived TLS certificates, and a Transit engine providing encryption as a service without exposing raw keys to applications.

Access is governed by identity-based policies evaluated against authentication methods including cloud IAM roles, Kubernetes service accounts, LDAP and GitHub, with every access recorded in an immutable audit log. Dynamic secrets minimize the lifetime of any single leaked credential compared to traditional static passwords.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Vault Community Edition is free, open source and self-hosted, with no secret-count limits, released under the Business Source License 1.1 since August 2023. HCP Vault Dedicated offers a managed SaaS Development tier around 0.03 dollars per hour, with production-grade Essentials and Standard tiers priced hourly plus a per-authenticated-client fee.

Vault Enterprise is a self-managed license for organizations needing full deployment control, adding the Transform secrets engine, disaster recovery replication and Sentinel governance; pricing is not published and commonly starts in the low six figures annually depending on scale.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source GraphQL engine that instantly generates high-performance GraphQL and REST APIs on top of databases with built-in permissions and real-time subscriptions.
enrichment.seo_title: Hasura Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hasura is an open-source GraphQL engine that instantly generates APIs on your database. See its features, pricing plans, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hasura is an open-source GraphQL engine that connects directly to a database, introspects its schema, and instantly generates a complete, high-performance GraphQL (and REST) API with built-in fine-grained permissions, real-time subscriptions and webhook triggers, removing much of the backend boilerplate developers traditionally write by hand to expose data to client applications. The core insight behind Hasura is architectural: instead of resolving a GraphQL query field by field in application code, which commonly causes the notorious N+1 query problem where a single GraphQL request triggers dozens of small database round trips, Hasura's engine compiles an incoming GraphQL query directly into a single, optimized SQL statement, regardless of how deeply nested the query is. That compiler-based approach is a major reason Hasura earned a reputation for extremely low latency and low resource consumption, with early benchmarks showing it serving over 1,000 requests per second on roughly 50 megabytes of RAM on a free-tier cloud instance. Hasura was founded in 2017 by Tanmai Gopal and Rajoshi Ghosh, and the company operates out of San Francisco with a significant engineering presence in Bangalore, India, reflecting the founders' Indian roots and the broader distributed nature of its team. The company has raised approximately 239 million dollars in funding from investors including Nexus Venture Partners, Lightspeed Venture Partners and Vertex Ventures, with a landmark 100 million dollar Series C round in February 2022 led by Greenoaks Capital and Nexus Venture Partners that valued the company at approximately 1 billion dollars, making Hasura one of the relatively small number of developer-tools startups from that era to reach unicorn status. The open-source core, the Hasura GraphQL Engine, remains available on GitHub under an Apache 2.0 license and has become one of the most widely adopted tools in its category, commonly cited alongside PostGraphile and Prisma as leading solutions for automatically generating APIs from relational databases, particularly PostgreSQL, though Hasura has since expanded connectivity to MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, MongoDB, Snowflake, BigQuery and other data sources through its Data Connector framework. Beyond automatic CRUD generation, Hasura's feature set includes row-level and column-level permission rules that can incorporate dynamic variables from an organization's existing authentication system (such as a JWT claim identifying the current user's role or tenant), real-time GraphQL subscriptions that push live updates to connected clients as underlying data changes, event triggers that fire webhooks on database insert, update or delete operations to integrate with external services, scheduled and cron triggers for background jobs, Actions for wiring custom business logic written in any language into the unified GraphQL schema, and Remote Schemas and Remote Joins that let Hasura federate multiple GraphQL APIs and data sources into a single unified endpoint. This makes Hasura useful both as a standalone backend for greenfield applications and as an API gateway layer that unifies existing databases, microservices and third-party GraphQL APIs behind one schema. Hasura is available in three primary forms. The open-source Hasura GraphQL Engine can be self-hosted for free with no licensing cost, giving full access to the core API generation, permissions and real-time capabilities on the infrastructure of the user's choosing. Hasura Cloud, the managed offering, starts with a free tier requiring no credit card, supporting up to 3 million API requests per month, 100 megabytes of data passthrough, up to three projects and two data connectors, positioned for hobby projects, testing and early-stage applications. The Cloud Professional tier moves to pay-as-you-go usage-based pricing billed monthly in arrears, charging 1.50 dollars per active-hour for generic Postgres-backed projects and 3.00 dollars per active-hour for advanced database connectors such as MongoDB, Snowflake or BigQuery, plus 0.13 dollars per gigabyte of data passthrough, and adds query caching, read replica support and observability features, aimed at low-to-moderate-scale production APIs. Cloud Enterprise sits above that with unlimited API requests, dedicated infrastructure, VPC peering, single sign-on, compliance certifications and 24/7 expert technical support, sold under a custom annual contract that typically requires a minimum 12-month commitment paid upfront, aimed at mission-critical or high-scale, compliance-heavy deployments. Hasura's target audience spans individual developers and startups building an API layer quickly on top of Postgres or another supported database, through to enterprises that need a governed, permissioned GraphQL gateway unifying multiple backend systems for web and mobile clients. Its closest competitors include PostGraphile (also Postgres-focused and open source), Prisma (a TypeScript-first ORM and data layer with its own API tooling), Supabase (which bundles a Postgres backend, auth and storage with its own auto-generated API layer), and Apollo Server for teams building GraphQL resolvers by hand; Hasura differentiates primarily through its compiler-based performance advantage, its broad multi-database connector support beyond just Postgres, and the maturity of its permissions and real-time subscription system relative to newer entrants in the instant-API space.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hasura is an open-source GraphQL engine that connects to a database and instantly generates a complete GraphQL and REST API with built-in permissions, real-time subscriptions and webhook triggers. It was founded in 2017 by Tanmai Gopal and Rajoshi Ghosh and is headquartered in San Francisco with a major engineering presence in Bangalore, India.

Instead of resolving GraphQL queries field by field, Hasura compiles incoming queries directly into a single optimized SQL statement, avoiding the N+1 query problem common in hand-written GraphQL resolvers and giving Hasura a reputation for very low latency and low resource usage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hasura automatically generates CRUD operations, real-time subscriptions and fine-grained row and column-level permissions from a database schema, with permission rules able to reference dynamic variables from an organization's existing authentication system. Event triggers fire webhooks on database changes, and scheduled or cron triggers support background jobs.

Actions let teams wire custom business logic written in any language into the unified GraphQL schema, while Remote Schemas and Remote Joins federate multiple GraphQL APIs and external data sources into one endpoint. Beyond PostgreSQL, Hasura's Data Connector framework extends support to MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, MongoDB, Snowflake and BigQuery.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The open-source Hasura GraphQL Engine is free to self-host with no licensing cost. Hasura Cloud's Free tier requires no credit card and supports up to 3 million API requests per month across up to three projects.

The Cloud Professional tier uses pay-as-you-go pricing at 1.50 dollars per active-hour for Postgres projects and 3.00 dollars per active-hour for advanced database connectors, plus 0.13 dollars per gigabyte of data passthrough. Cloud Enterprise offers unlimited requests, dedicated infrastructure and 24/7 support under a custom annual contract requiring a minimum 12-month upfront commitment.

faqs.question: Is Hasura free to use

faqs.question: Does Hasura only work with PostgreSQL
faqs.answer: No, while PostgreSQL was Hasura's original focus, its Data Connector framework now supports MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, MongoDB, Snowflake and BigQuery, among other data sources.

faqs.question: Who founded Hasura

faqs.answer: Hasura compiles an incoming GraphQL query directly into a single optimized SQL statement rather than resolving fields one at a time, avoiding the multiple round-trip database queries that cause the N+1 problem in hand-written resolvers.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, fully automated ActivityPub bridge written in Rust that lets any static site federate with the Fediverse without adding a dynamic backend.
enrichment.seo_title: Hatsu Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hatsu is an open-source, self-hosted ActivityPub bridge for static sites. See its features, setup requirements, pros, cons and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hatsu is a self-hosted, fully automated ActivityPub bridge that lets any static website participate in the Fediverse, the decentralized social network built on the ActivityPub protocol that underpins platforms like Mastodon and GoToSocial, without requiring the site itself to run a dynamic backend or implement federation logic. Static site generators such as Hugo, Jekyll, Eleventy and Astro produce plain HTML files with no server-side application to handle incoming ActivityPub requests, follow relationships, or replies from Fediverse users, so historically the only ways to federate a static site were either to bolt on a separate dynamic service or to implement Webmention-based interoperability manually, both of which add operational complexity. Hatsu solves this by running as its own lightweight server process alongside (or in front of) the static site: it reads the site's existing content feed in JSON Feed, Atom or RSS format, rather than requiring the site to add microformats2 markup to its HTML, and from that feed it automatically creates and maintains ActivityPub actor profiles that Fediverse users can discover and follow. When someone on Mastodon or another ActivityPub-compatible platform searches for a page on the underlying static site or for one of its authors, Hatsu transparently redirects that search to the corresponding Hatsu-hosted profile or post, handles the incoming follow request, distributes new posts to followers automatically when the site's feed updates, and, critically, backfeeds replies from Fediverse users to the static site so that Fediverse-native conversation threads can be surfaced back on the original site. All of this happens without the site owner needing to manually send or process Webmentions, and without modifying the static site's HTML templates to include federation markup, since Hatsu relies entirely on the machine-readable feed as its data source. The project is developed by a GitHub user and maintainer known as importantimport, and is hosted publicly at github.com/importantimport/hatsu; it is written primarily in Rust (accounting for the large majority of the codebase), which the project cites as a deliberate choice for performance and for enabling deployment on lower-powered hardware, including aarch64-based devices, alongside straightforward Docker-based self-hosting. Hatsu builds on the Activitypub-Federation-Rust library, the same underlying Rust ActivityPub implementation used by Lemmy, the federated link-aggregation platform, which gives Hatsu a battle-tested foundation for handling the cryptographic HTTP signature verification and federation protocol details that ActivityPub requires for secure, spoofing-resistant communication between servers. The project explicitly supports compatibility features such as Mastodon's secure mode and GoToSocial interoperability, and documents redirecting via both static files and markup for site owners who want more control over how their pages resolve to Hatsu profiles. As of mid-2026 the project has accumulated a modest but active following on GitHub, with around 256 stars, 8 forks and 33 tagged releases, the most recent in December 2025, indicating a small, focused, actively maintained open-source project rather than a large commercial product. Hatsu is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), a copyleft license that requires anyone who modifies and distributes the software, including running a modified version as a network service, to make their source code changes available, consistent with its lineage from the AGPL-licensed Lemmy codebase. Because it is entirely open source with no company, funding or commercial entity behind it, Hatsu has no pricing tiers, subscription plans or paid support offering; it is free to download, self-host and modify, with the only cost being the server infrastructure an operator chooses to run it on and the time required to configure DNS, reverse proxying and the feed source correctly. Hatsu's target audience is technically inclined bloggers, independent publishers and static-site-generator users who want their content and its authors to be discoverable and followable on Mastodon and other Fediverse platforms without abandoning the simplicity, cost and performance benefits of a fully static site. Its closest points of comparison are Bridgy Fed, a hosted (non-self-hosted) service that performs a broadly similar function of bridging websites, blogs and other protocols into the Fediverse, and WordPress's official ActivityPub plugin, which serves a similar purpose but only for WordPress-powered dynamic sites; Hatsu differentiates by being self-hosted rather than a third-party hosted dependency, by working from any feed-producing static site generator rather than being tied to one platform, and by being written in Rust for a lightweight, low-resource footprint suitable for running on modest self-hosted infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hatsu is a self-hosted, fully automated ActivityPub bridge that lets static websites federate with the Fediverse, the decentralized social network underlying platforms like Mastodon and GoToSocial, without adding a dynamic backend to the site itself. It reads a site's existing JSON Feed, Atom or RSS feed and uses that as its data source, rather than requiring HTML markup changes.

The project is written primarily in Rust, built on the Activitypub-Federation-Rust library also used by Lemmy, and is maintained by a GitHub developer known as importantimport under the AGPLv3 open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hatsu automatically creates ActivityPub actor profiles from a site's feed, redirects Fediverse searches for site pages or authors to the corresponding Hatsu-hosted profile, and accepts and distributes follower relationships as the feed updates with new posts.

It backfeeds replies from Fediverse users to the static site so Fediverse conversations can surface on the original content, implements HTTP signature verification for secure federation, and supports compatibility modes for Mastodon's secure mode and GoToSocial. Deployment is Docker-based for straightforward self-hosting, with aarch64 support for lower-powered hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hatsu is entirely free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, with no company, subscription plans or paid tiers behind it.

The only costs involved are the server infrastructure an operator chooses to self-host Hatsu on and the time needed to configure DNS, reverse proxying and the feed source, since there is no managed hosting option or commercial support offering.

faqs.question: What is Hatsu used for
faqs.answer: Hatsu is used to let a static website participate in the Fediverse, allowing Mastodon and other ActivityPub users to follow the site, see new posts, and reply, without the site needing a dynamic backend.

faqs.question: Is Hatsu free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Hatsu is completely free and open source under the AGPLv3 license. There are no subscription plans; operators only pay for the server infrastructure they self-host it on.

faqs.question: Does Hatsu work with any static site generator
faqs.answer: Yes, Hatsu works with any static site that produces a JSON Feed, Atom or RSS feed, including sites built with Hugo, Jekyll, Eleventy and Astro, since it reads the feed rather than requiring specific HTML markup.

faqs.question: What programming language is Hatsu written in
faqs.answer: Hatsu is written primarily in Rust, built on the Activitypub-Federation-Rust library, the same library used by the federated platform Lemmy.

faqs.question: Does Hatsu support protocols other than ActivityPub
faqs.answer: No, Hatsu is ActivityPub-only and does not bridge to other decentralized protocols such as Nostr or AT Protocol (Bluesky).

faqs.question: How is Hatsu different from Bridgy Fed
faqs.answer: Hatsu is self-hosted, giving operators full control over their own instance, while Bridgy Fed is a hosted third-party service that performs a broadly similar bridging function without requiring self-hosting.
